title: Adding a Temporal Dimension to Diffusion
concept: Video diffusion models add temporal layers (callback to Unit 191's 3D convolutions) that let information flow across frames, not just within one frame.

exercises.prompt: When you flip through a photo album fast, how do you know it's a moving story and not random pictures?

options.text: Each photo connects smoothly to the next, showing change over time

exercises.explain: Right! A video is really just images that stay consistent and flow smoothly from one moment to the next — that's exactly what temporal layers help AI understand.

cards.title: A Single Photo Isn't a Movie
cards.body: An image diffusion model can paint one gorgeous frame. But stitch a bunch of AI frames together randomly and you get a flickering mess, not a movie! To make real video, AI needs a way to remember what happened in the last frame and the one before that. That's the missing ingredient: time.

cards.title: Temporal Layers
cards.body: Temporal layers are special parts inside a video diffusion model that let information flow ACROSS frames, not just within a single frame. They connect frame 1 to frame 2 to frame 3, so objects move smoothly and stay consistent instead of randomly changing each frame.

cards.title: The Relay Race Baton
cards.body: Think of each video frame as a runner in a relay race. Without a baton pass, each runner would just start running in a totally new random direction! The temporal layer is the baton — it carries information from one runner (frame) to the next, so the whole race (video) looks like one continuous motion instead of separate disconnected sprints.

cards.title: A Dog Running Across the Screen
cards.body: Imagine generating a video of a dog running. The image layers figure out what the dog looks like in each single frame. The temporal layers make sure the dog's legs move in a believable pattern from frame to frame, and that the dog keeps moving in the same direction instead of teleporting around randomly.

cards.title: Myth: Video AI Just Makes Lots of Pictures
cards.body: Myth: A video diffusion model is basically an image model that just runs many times to make separate pictures. Truth: If you only generated separate frames, they wouldn't match up — objects would flicker and jump around. Temporal layers are specifically added so frames talk to each other and stay consistent over time.

cards.title: Spatial vs Temporal Layers
cards.body: Video diffusion models mix two kinds of layers: ones that look WITHIN a frame, and ones that look ACROSS frames.

left.text: Look at pixels within ONE frame — shapes, colors, textures in that single image

right.text: Look ACROSS frames over time — motion, consistency, smooth change from frame to frame

exercises.prompt: What is the main job of a temporal layer in a video diffusion model?

options.text: To make each frame more colorful
To let information flow across frames so motion looks smooth and consistent
To compress the video file size

exercises.explain: Temporal layers connect frames together over time, which is exactly what makes motion in AI-generated video look smooth instead of flickery.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Without temporal layers, generating each frame separately would likely cause objects to flicker or jump inconsistently between frames.
exercises.explain: True! Each frame would be generated somewhat independently, so without a connection across time, things like a character's shirt color or position could randomly change frame to frame.

exercises.sentence: Temporal layers let information flow ___ frames, not just within one frame.
exercises.explain: 'Across' is correct — temporal layers connect information between frames over time, which is the key idea of this lesson.

exercises.prompt: Spot the AI: Which video below is MOST LIKELY missing working temporal layers?

options.text: A video where a person's shirt randomly changes color every single frame
A video where a person walks smoothly and consistently across the screen
A video where a ball bounces in a believable, continuous arc

exercises.explain: Random flickering details like a shirt changing color every frame is a classic sign that frames aren't being connected properly over time — a job for temporal layers.

exercises.prompt: This lesson connected back to Unit 191's 3D convolutions. Why would 3D convolutions (which look at width, height, AND time) be useful for video diffusion's temporal layers?

options.text: Because 3D convolutions can process depth (time) alongside the normal image dimensions, helping connect frames together
Because 3D convolutions make videos load faster on phones
Because 3D convolutions only work on black and white videos
Because 3D convolutions replace the need for any image layers at all

exercises.explain: Exactly! Just like 3D convolutions extend 2D convolutions by adding a time dimension, temporal layers extend image diffusion by adding the ability to relate frames across time — same core idea, applied to video generation.
